content.explanation:
Event handling in React is similar to handling events in plain HTML/JavaScript, but with some syntactic differences. In HTML, you write `onclick="handleClick()"` as a string attribute. In React JSX, you write `onClick={handleClick}` — the event name is camelCase, and you pass a function reference (not a string). This is a fundamental part of making React components interactive.

The most common events you will work with are `onClick` for buttons and clickable elements, `onChange` for input fields, selects, and textareas, and `onSubmit` for forms. React supports all standard DOM events with the same camelCase naming convention: `onMouseEnter`, `onKeyDown`, `onFocus`, `onBlur`, `onScroll`, and many more. Each event handler receives a SyntheticEvent object as its argument.

There are two common patterns for defining event handlers. The first is defining a named function and passing its reference: `function handleClick() { ... }` then `<button onClick={handleClick}>`. The second is using inline arrow functions: `<button onClick={() => console.log('clicked')}>`. Named functions are preferred for readability and because they can be reused. Inline functions are convenient for simple one-liners or when you need to pass arguments.

A critical distinction is between passing a function reference and calling a function. `onClick={handleClick}` passes the function to be called later when the event occurs. `onClick={handleClick()}` calls the function immediately during rendering and passes its return value as the handler — this is almost always a bug. If you need to pass arguments, use an arrow function wrapper: `onClick={() => handleClick(id)}`.

Event handlers in React follow a naming convention: handler functions are typically named `handleX` where X describes the event (handleClick, handleChange, handleSubmit). When passed as props, they are named `onX` (onClick, onChange, onSubmit). This convention makes code easy to read: `<Button onClick={handleClick} />` clearly shows that clicking the Button triggers handleClick.

codeExamples.title: Basic Event Handler Patterns
codeExamples.output:
Button clicked!
Mouse entered!
Value changed to: Hello React
codeExamples.explanation: Event handlers are plain functions. Named functions are preferred for readability. Each handler receives an event object with details about what triggered the event.

codeExamples.title: Function Reference vs Function Call
codeExamples.output:
Type of correct handler: function
Clicked!
Type of wrong handler: string
Deleting item: 42
codeExamples.explanation: Pass `handleClick` (reference) not `handleClick()` (call). To pass arguments, wrap in an arrow function: `() => handleClick(arg)`.

codeExamples.title: Common Event Types
codeExamples.output:
onClick: Button pressed
onChange: React
onSubmit: Form submitted
codeExamples.explanation: onClick handles clicks, onChange handles input value changes, and onSubmit handles form submissions. Each receives a synthetic event object.

notes: React event names use camelCase: `onClick`, `onChange`, `onSubmit` — not `onclick`, `onchange`, `onsubmit`.
Always pass a function reference to event handlers, not a function call. Use `onClick={handleClick}` not `onClick={handleClick()}`.
Follow the naming convention: handler functions use `handle` prefix (handleClick), and callback props use `on` prefix (onClick).
commonPitfalls: Writing `onClick={handleClick()}` instead of `onClick={handleClick}` — the parentheses cause the function to execute immediately during render, not on click.

Forgetting that `onChange` in React fires on every keystroke, not just when the input loses focus (which is the behavior of the native `change` event). This is actually React's `onInput` behavior mapped to the `onChange` name for consistency.

exercises.title: Create Event Handlers
exercises.description: Create an event handler system that simulates button clicks and input changes. Implement handleClick and handleChange functions that process mock events.
exercises.expectedOutput:
Button: Save
Button: Cancel
Input: React
Input: React is great
hints: Event handlers receive an event object. Access the data through `event.target` which contains properties of the element that triggered the event.
For click events, read `event.target.label`. For change events, read `event.target.value`.

exercises.title: Event Handlers with Arguments
exercises.description: Create a handler factory that generates event handlers for a list of items. Each handler should know which item it belongs to.
exercises.expectedOutput:
Selected: Apple
Selected: Banana
Selected: Cherry
hints: createHandler is a function that returns another function (a closure). The inner function captures itemName from the outer scope.
This is the same pattern as `onClick={() => handleSelect(item)}` in JSX — the arrow function closes over the item variable.

content.explanation:
React wraps the browser's native events in its own event system called SyntheticEvent. When you handle an event in React, you are not working with the browser's native event directly — you are working with a React-created wrapper object that has the same interface. SyntheticEvent normalizes event behavior across different browsers, ensuring your event handling code works consistently regardless of whether the user is on Chrome, Firefox, Safari, or Edge.

A SyntheticEvent object has the same properties and methods you would find on a native event: `target`, `currentTarget`, `type`, `preventDefault()`, `stopPropagation()`, and more. The `target` property refers to the DOM element that triggered the event. The `currentTarget` refers to the element the handler is attached to (they differ when events bubble). You can call `event.preventDefault()` to prevent default browser behavior (like form submission) and `event.stopPropagation()` to prevent the event from bubbling up.

Historically, React used event pooling for performance — the SyntheticEvent object was reused across events, and all properties were nullified after the handler returned. This meant you could not access event properties asynchronously (like in a setTimeout). Starting with React 17, event pooling was removed, so this is no longer an issue. However, it is still a common interview topic because it was a significant gotcha in older React versions.

If you need access to the underlying native browser event, you can use `event.nativeEvent`. This is rarely necessary but can be useful when working with browser APIs that require native events, such as certain drag-and-drop implementations or when integrating with non-React libraries. In general, stick with the SyntheticEvent interface for consistency.

React uses event delegation under the hood. Instead of attaching event listeners to every individual DOM node, React attaches a single listener to the root of your app (the root DOM container since React 17, previously the document). When an event occurs, React determines which component it belongs to and calls the appropriate handler. This delegation system is more memory-efficient than attaching individual listeners and is transparent to the developer.

codeExamples.title: SyntheticEvent Properties
codeExamples.output:
Type: click
Target tag: BUTTON
Target id: submit-btn
Default prevented: true
codeExamples.explanation: SyntheticEvents wrap native events with a consistent interface. They provide target, type, preventDefault(), stopPropagation(), and all standard event properties.

codeExamples.title: Event Target vs CurrentTarget
codeExamples.output:
Clicked on: BUTTON
Handler on: BUTTON
Same element: true
---
Clicked on: SPAN
Handler on: DIV
Same element: false
codeExamples.explanation: event.target is what the user actually clicked. event.currentTarget is the element with the event handler. They differ when events bubble from child to parent.

codeExamples.title: preventDefault and stopPropagation
codeExamples.output:
Form submit prevented, handling with JS
Default prevented: true
---
Without stopPropagation:
Outer clicked
With stopPropagation:
Inner clicked, stopped propagation
codeExamples.explanation: preventDefault() stops default browser behavior (form submission, link navigation). stopPropagation() prevents the event from reaching parent handlers.

notes: SyntheticEvents provide a consistent API across all browsers. You do not need to worry about browser-specific event behavior.
Event pooling was removed in React 17. You can now safely access event properties asynchronously in modern React.
React uses event delegation — a single listener on the root container handles all events, making it more efficient than individual DOM listeners.
commonPitfalls: Confusing `event.target` with `event.currentTarget` — target is the element that was actually clicked, while currentTarget is the element the handler is attached to. This matters when handling events on parent elements.

Calling `event.stopPropagation()` unnecessarily, which can prevent other parts of your application (or third-party libraries) from receiving events they depend on. Only stop propagation when you have a specific reason.
interviewTips: Explain that SyntheticEvent is React's cross-browser wrapper around native events. Mention that React 17 changed event delegation from document to the root container and removed event pooling.
Be ready to explain the difference between event.target and event.currentTarget, and when to use preventDefault() vs stopPropagation().

exercises.title: Build a SyntheticEvent Simulator
exercises.description: Create a function that simulates React's SyntheticEvent by wrapping a native event object with additional methods and normalized properties.
exercises.expectedOutput:
Type: click
Target: BUTTON
Has native: true
Prevented: true
Stopped: true
hints: Return an object that copies type and target from nativeEvent and adds the methods preventDefault and stopPropagation.
Use `this` inside the methods to modify properties on the returned object. Store a reference to nativeEvent so it can be accessed later.

exercises.title: Event Delegation Simulation
exercises.description: Simulate React's event delegation pattern. Create a root handler that dispatches events to the correct component handler based on the target element.
exercises.expectedOutput:
Saving...
Deleting...
Cancelled
No handler for: unknown
hints: Create an object where keys are element IDs and values are handler functions. The rootHandler looks up the correct handler by event.target.id.
This simulates how React attaches a single listener to the root and dispatches to the correct component handler based on the event target.

content.explanation:
A very common need in React is passing extra data to an event handler. For example, when rendering a list of items, each item's delete button needs to know which item to delete. Since event handlers receive only the event object, you need a way to include additional information. There are several patterns to accomplish this, each with different tradeoffs.

The most common and recommended approach is wrapping the handler in an arrow function: `onClick={() => handleDelete(item.id)}`. The arrow function creates a closure that captures the `item.id` value. When the user clicks, the arrow function executes and calls `handleDelete` with the captured argument. This is clean, readable, and the pattern you will see in almost every React codebase.

Another approach is using `Function.prototype.bind`: `onClick={handleDelete.bind(null, item.id)}`. The `bind` method creates a new function with preset arguments. The first argument to bind is the `this` context (null for regular functions), and subsequent arguments are prepended to the handler's arguments. So `handleDelete.bind(null, 42)` creates a function that, when called, invokes `handleDelete(42, event)`. This approach was more common before arrow functions were standard.

A third pattern is creating a handler factory — a function that returns a handler function. For example, `const createDeleteHandler = (id) => () => handleDelete(id)`. You use it as `onClick={createDeleteHandler(item.id)}`. This is useful when you want to define the handler logic outside of JSX or when the same handler pattern is used in multiple places. The factory creates a closure over the id parameter.

A performance consideration: when using inline arrow functions in JSX, a new function is created on every render. In most cases, this has negligible performance impact. However, in performance-critical situations (like rendering thousands of list items), you might want to use `useCallback` or handler factories to avoid unnecessary re-creation. For beginners, do not worry about this optimization — use inline arrows freely and optimize only when profiling shows a bottleneck.

codeExamples.title: Arrow Function Pattern
codeExamples.output:
Deleting item: 1
Deleting item: 2
Deleting item: 3
---
Editing Banana (id: 2)
codeExamples.explanation: Arrow functions in JSX create closures that capture the current value of variables. Each iteration captures its own `item`, so each handler passes the correct ID.

codeExamples.title: Bind Method Pattern
codeExamples.output:
Delete item 1 (event: click)
Edit item 2 (event: click)
Delete item 3 (event: click)
codeExamples.explanation: bind creates a new function with preset arguments. The event object is appended after the bound arguments. Arrow functions achieve the same result with clearer syntax.

codeExamples.title: Handler Factory Pattern
codeExamples.output:
DELETE: 1
DELETE: 2
ARCHIVE: 1
Clicked item: 10
Clicked item: 20
Clicked item: 30
codeExamples.explanation: Handler factories return specialized functions through closures. This is useful for creating reusable handler patterns with curried arguments.

notes: Arrow functions in event handlers are the most common pattern in React. They are clean, readable, and easy to understand.
The `bind` approach is functionally equivalent to arrow functions but less readable. It was more popular before ES6 arrow functions.
Creating a new function on every render (via arrow functions or bind) is rarely a performance issue. Only optimize with useCallback when profiling shows a real bottleneck.
commonPitfalls: Calling the function immediately instead of wrapping it: `onClick={handleDelete(id)}` runs on render. Use `onClick={() => handleDelete(id)}` to defer execution until the click event.

Forgetting that arrow functions in JSX create a new function reference on every render. This can cause issues if the handler is passed to a memoized child component, because the new reference breaks memoization.

exercises.title: List Item Handlers
exercises.description: Create a system where each item in a list has its own click handler that logs the item's data. Use arrow functions to pass the item information.
exercises.expectedOutput:
Selected: Laptop ($999) [p1]
Selected: Phone ($699) [p2]
Selected: Tablet ($499) [p3]
hints: For each product, create an arrow function that calls handleProductClick with that product's specific data.
Use forEach to iterate over products. Inside the loop, create a handler with `() => handleProductClick(product.id, product.name, product.price)` and call it.

exercises.title: Create a Handler Factory
exercises.description: Build a handler factory function that generates different types of action handlers (add, remove, update) for different resource types.
hints: createActionHandler takes two arguments and returns a new function. The returned function takes an id and uses all three values in the log message.
The inner function forms a closure over resourceType and action, so it can access them even after createActionHandler has returned.

content.explanation:
Forms are a fundamental part of web applications, and React provides a powerful pattern for handling them called controlled components. In a controlled component, form input values are driven by React state rather than the DOM. The input's value is set by state, and every change to the input updates the state via an onChange handler. This gives React full control over the form data and enables real-time validation, dynamic formatting, and conditional form behavior.

The `onSubmit` event handler is attached to the `<form>` element and fires when the user presses Enter in an input or clicks a submit button. The first thing you should do in most onSubmit handlers is call `event.preventDefault()` — this prevents the browser's default form submission behavior, which would cause a full page reload. Instead, you handle the submission in JavaScript, typically sending the data to an API via fetch.

A controlled input follows this pattern: the input's `value` attribute is bound to a state variable, and the `onChange` handler updates that state. For example: `<input value={name} onChange={(e) => setName(e.target.value)} />`. This means React is the single source of truth for the input's value. Without the onChange handler, the input would appear frozen because the user's keystrokes would not update the state that controls the value.

For forms with multiple fields, you can either use separate useState calls for each field or a single useState with an object. The object approach uses a single handler: `const handleChange = (e) => setForm({...form, [e.target.name]: e.target.value})`. The computed property name `[e.target.name]` dynamically updates the correct field based on the input's name attribute. This scales better than individual state variables for large forms.

Input validation in controlled forms is straightforward because you have access to the current value at all times. You can validate on every keystroke (showing real-time errors), on blur (when the user leaves the field), or on submit (all at once). Controlled inputs also make it easy to implement features like character counting, auto-formatting (phone numbers, credit cards), and conditional field visibility based on other field values.

codeExamples.title: Controlled Input Pattern
codeExamples.output:
After H: H
After He: He
After Hello: Hello
codeExamples.explanation: In a controlled input, every keystroke triggers onChange, which updates the state. The state drives the input's displayed value, creating a two-way sync.

codeExamples.title: Form Submission with preventDefault
codeExamples.output:
Submitting: alice@test.com
Errors: Invalid email, Password too short
codeExamples.explanation: Always call preventDefault() in form submit handlers to prevent page reload. Then validate the data and either show errors or submit to an API.

codeExamples.title: Multi-Field Form with Object State
codeExamples.output:
Name: Alice
Email: alice@test.com
Role: admin
codeExamples.explanation: Using `[event.target.name]` as a computed property key, one handler manages all form fields. Each input needs a `name` attribute matching the state key.

notes: Always call `event.preventDefault()` in onSubmit handlers to prevent the browser from reloading the page.
Controlled inputs require both a `value` prop and an `onChange` handler. Without onChange, the input appears frozen to the user.
For forms with many fields, use a single state object and a generic handler with computed property names: `[e.target.name]: e.target.value`.
commonPitfalls: Forgetting `event.preventDefault()` in the form submit handler — the page will reload and you will lose all React state and component state.
Setting a `value` prop on an input without an `onChange` handler — this makes the input read-only because React controls the value but nothing updates it on user input.

exercises.title: Build a Controlled Form
exercises.description: Create a form state manager that handles multiple inputs. Implement handleChange for updating individual fields and handleSubmit for validating and logging the final data.
exercises.expectedOutput:
Invalid: username too short, invalid email, invalid age
Valid: Alice (alice@mail.com, age 25)
hints: Use spread syntax with computed property names to update a specific field: `form = { ...form, [name]: value }`.
For validation, check each condition and push error messages to an array. If the array is empty, the form is valid.

exercises.title: Real-Time Validation
exercises.description: Create a password validation system that checks multiple rules on every keystroke and reports which rules pass and fail.
exercises.expectedOutput:
--- Testing "hello" ---
FAIL: At least 8 characters
FAIL: Contains uppercase letter
FAIL: Contains a number
All passed: false
--- Testing "Hello123!" ---
PASS: At least 8 characters
PASS: Contains uppercase letter
PASS: Contains a number
All passed: true
hints: Iterate over the rules array and call each rule's test function with the password. Log the result with PASS or FAIL prefix.
Track whether all rules pass using a boolean variable that starts as true and gets set to false when any rule fails.

content.explanation:
Beyond click and change events, React provides handlers for a full range of keyboard and mouse interactions. Keyboard events are essential for building accessible interfaces, implementing shortcuts, and handling special keys. Mouse events enable hover effects, tooltips, drag interactions, and context menus. Understanding these events lets you build rich, interactive UIs.

Keyboard events in React include `onKeyDown` (fires when a key is pressed), `onKeyUp` (fires when a key is released), and `onKeyPress` (deprecated, do not use). The event object for keyboard events includes `event.key` (the key value like 'Enter', 'Escape', 'a'), `event.code` (the physical key like 'KeyA', 'Enter'), and modifier properties `event.ctrlKey`, `event.shiftKey`, `event.altKey`, and `event.metaKey` (Cmd on Mac). Always use `event.key` for logical key detection and `event.code` for physical key position.

Common keyboard patterns include detecting Enter to submit, Escape to close modals, and keyboard shortcuts like Ctrl+S to save. When implementing keyboard shortcuts, check modifier keys alongside the main key: `if (event.ctrlKey && event.key === 's') { event.preventDefault(); handleSave(); }`. The `preventDefault()` is important here to stop the browser's default Ctrl+S save dialog from appearing.

Mouse events include `onMouseEnter` and `onMouseLeave` (for hover effects without event bubbling), `onMouseOver` and `onMouseOut` (with bubbling), `onMouseDown` and `onMouseUp` (for press and release), and `onMouseMove` (for tracking position). The mouse event object includes `event.clientX` and `event.clientY` (coordinates relative to viewport), `event.pageX` and `event.pageY` (relative to document), and `event.button` (which mouse button was used).

React's event delegation simplifies event handling across many elements. Instead of attaching listeners to each list item, you can attach one listener to the parent and use `event.target` to determine which child was interacted with. This is efficient and scales well. While React handles delegation internally (attaching to the root), you can also leverage this pattern explicitly for dynamic lists where items are added and removed frequently.

codeExamples.title: Keyboard Event Handling
codeExamples.output:
Key: Enter
Key: Ctrl+s
Key: Escape
Key: Ctrl+Shift+Z
codeExamples.explanation: Keyboard events provide the key name and modifier states. Use these to implement keyboard shortcuts and handle special keys like Enter and Escape.

codeExamples.title: Mouse Event Tracking
codeExamples.output:
Mouse entered: card-1
Position: (150, 200)
Position: (155, 205)
Mouse left: card-1
codeExamples.explanation: onMouseEnter/Leave track hover state. onMouseMove provides real-time cursor coordinates for dynamic effects like tooltips or custom cursors.

codeExamples.title: Event Delegation for Lists
codeExamples.output:
edit item #1
delete item #2
view item #3
Clicked outside action area
codeExamples.explanation: Instead of attaching handlers to every item, one handler on the parent uses event.target data attributes to determine which item and action was triggered.

notes: Use `onKeyDown` instead of the deprecated `onKeyPress`. Check `event.key` for the key value and modifier properties (ctrlKey, shiftKey, etc.) for combinations.
Use `onMouseEnter`/`onMouseLeave` (no bubbling) for hover effects. Use `onMouseOver`/`onMouseOut` only when you need bubbling behavior.
Event delegation on a parent element is an efficient pattern for handling interactions on dynamic lists with many children.
commonPitfalls: Forgetting `event.preventDefault()` when handling keyboard shortcuts like Ctrl+S — the browser's default behavior (save dialog) will fire alongside your handler.
Using `onMouseOver` and `onMouseOut` for hover effects — these bubble, so moving between child elements triggers unwanted events. Use `onMouseEnter` and `onMouseLeave` instead, which do not bubble.

exercises.title: Keyboard Shortcut Handler
exercises.description: Create a keyboard shortcut system that maps key combinations to actions and executes the correct action when a key event is received.
exercises.expectedOutput:
Action: save
Action: undo
Action: redo
Action: close
No shortcut
hints: Use Array.find() to search for a shortcut matching the event's key, ctrlKey, and shiftKey properties.
Handle the optional shift property with a default: `(s.shift || false)` ensures undefined becomes false for comparison.

exercises.title: Hover State Tracker
exercises.description: Build a hover tracking system that maintains which element is currently hovered and logs enter/leave transitions.
exercises.expectedOutput:
Enter: card-1
Hovered: card-1
Leave: card-1
Hovered: null
Enter: card-2
Hovered: card-2
Leave: card-2
Hovered: null
hints: handleEnter sets the hoveredElement variable to the given id and logs the entry. handleLeave sets it back to null.
This simulates how you would track hover state in React with onMouseEnter and onMouseLeave handlers updating a state variable.
